title: 132-Million-Year-Old Dinosaur Tracks Rewrite Southern Africa's History
summary:
A stunning discovery on a remote South African beach is forcing scientists to rethink the history of dinosaurs in southern Africa. In early 2025, a team of researchers exploring a small rock outcrop near Knysna stumbled upon something extraordinary: dinosaur tracks. Dozens of them. And these aren't just any tracks—they're about 132 million years old, making them the youngest dinosaur footprints ever found in the region.

The discovery happened almost by accident. The team, led by ichnologists (scientists who study fossil tracks and traces), was actually searching for a dinosaur tooth that a 13-year-old boy had found nearby in 2017. Instead, team member Linda Helm spotted the footprints. "We couldn't believe our eyes," she later said. The site, called the Brenton Formation, is tiny—no more than 40 meters long and 5 meters wide, with cliffs rising just 5 meters above the shore. Yet in that small area, the team found over two dozen possible footprints.

Why does this matter? Around 182 million years ago, massive volcanic eruptions spread lava across much of southern Africa, covering the Karoo Basin where many dinosaurs once lived. After that, the fossil record went quiet. Scientists assumed dinosaurs had disappeared from the region. But these tracks prove otherwise. They show that dinosaurs continued to thrive in southern Africa for tens of millions of years after the eruptions.

The tracks are also significant because they come from the Cretaceous period (145 to 66 million years ago), a time when the supercontinent Gondwana was breaking apart. Only a handful of dinosaur fossils from this period have been found in the Western Cape. These tracks are only the second set of Cretaceous dinosaur tracks ever found in South Africa.

Now, the race is on to study the tracks before they are destroyed. The site lies in the intertidal zone, meaning it's covered by seawater at high tide twice a day. The team plans to return during low tide to document and analyze the footprints in detail. Each track could reveal clues about the dinosaur that made it—its size, speed, and even its behavior.
why_it_matters: This discovery shows how a single find can challenge long-held scientific assumptions, reminding us that the past is not fully written – and that careful observation of overlooked places can unlock major secrets about Earth's history.

keywords.explanation: Scientists who study fossil footprints and other traces left by ancient animals.
The total collection of fossils that shows how life changed over millions of years.
The part of a seashore that is underwater at high tide and exposed at low tide.
The last era of the dinosaurs, from about 145 to 66 million years ago.
An ancient supercontinent that broke apart to form today's southern continents including Africa and South America.
A small area of bedrock that sticks out above the ground or water, where fossils might be found.

questions.question: How old are the dinosaur tracks found on the South African beach?
What was the team originally searching for when they found the tracks?
Why had scientists assumed dinosaurs disappeared from southern Africa after 182 million years ago?
options: Because of massive volcanic eruptions and a quiet fossil record
Because the area became an ocean
questions.correct_answer: Because of massive volcanic eruptions and a quiet fossil record

questions.question: What time period do these tracks come from?
How many sets of Cretaceous dinosaur tracks had been found in South Africa before this discovery?
What threatens to destroy the track site?

background_read: The discovery took place at the Brenton Formation near Knysna, a town in South Africa's Western Cape province known for its scenic coastline and fossil deposits.

The Karoo Basin, a large region in southern Africa, was once home to many dinosaurs before massive volcanic eruptions about 182 million years ago covered the area with lava, creating a gap in the fossil record.

The tracks date to the Cretaceous period (145–66 million years ago), when the supercontinent Gondwana was splitting into the continents we know today. This tectonic breakup created new landscapes and changed the habitats where dinosaurs lived.

Ichnology, the study of tracks and traces, is a crucial tool for understanding ancient life even when body fossils (bones) are missing. These tracks provide rare evidence of post-eruption dinosaurs in the region.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Accidental discovery of dinosaur tracks on a South African beach.
└─ Specific: Linda Helm spotted the footprints while the team searched for a dinosaur tooth.
LOCATION & SIZE: The Brenton Formation – only 40m long, 5m wide, 5m high from shore.
└─ Number of tracks: over two dozen.
HISTORICAL GAP: After massive volcanic eruptions 182 million years ago, the fossil record in the Karoo Basin went quiet, and scientists assumed dinosaurs vanished from southern Africa.
└─ Significance: Tracks are 132 million years old – the youngest in the region, proving dinosaurs survived tens of millions of years after the eruptions.
GEOLOGICAL CONTEXT: Cretaceous period, when Gondwana broke apart. Only the second set of Cretaceous dinosaur tracks ever found in South Africa.
URGENCY: The intertidal site is covered by seawater twice daily and could be eroded away.
└─ Plan: Return at low tide to document and analyze the footprints for size, speed, and behavior.

perspectives.description: Paleontologists gain a rare window into dinosaur survival after a major volcanic catastrophe, potentially rewriting regional extinction timelines and offering new data for comparative studies.
The track site faces imminent destruction from daily tidal erosion, threatening to erase evidence before it can be fully documented, highlighting the vulnerability of coastal fossil sites.
The tracks are not body fossils but trace fossils, limiting the type of information available (e.g., no DNA, no bone). Nevertheless, they provide behavioral and ecological clues that bones cannot.

Researchers plan to use photogrammetry and 3D scanning to digitally preserve the tracks. This case could inspire similar re-examinations of other 'dead zones' in the fossil record, changing how we interpret gaps in ancient life.
